coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of NaTaO3 nanoparticles and fabrication of nanostructured NaTaO3 for detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Pallavi, K.M.
Mamatha, G.P.
Nagaraju, G.
Soundarya, T.L.
coredata.dc:description: Herein a novel electrochemical sensor based on NaTaO3 (NTO) nanoparticles (NPs) immobilized modified GCE is fabricated for sensitive and selective determination of dopamine in the presence of Ascorbic acid. The perovskite NTO NPs were synthesized from the facile hydrothermal method. The structural morphology and characterization of NTO NPs were examined by using powder XRD, FT-IR, UV-DRS, SEM, elemental mapping, EDS, and HR-TEM analysis. NTO NPs showed a fantastic synergistic impact on the electrochemical sensing of dopamine (DA) at very low concentrations. Under optimum conditions, The NTO NPs/MGCE exhibited good linear properties in the concentration range from 5 × 10-7 M to 5 µM for the quantitative analysis of DA with a limit of detection 0.17 µM. The developed NTO NPs/MGCE could provide a new platform for the routine study of DA and Ascorbic acid (AA) due to its outstanding reproducibility and repeatability, good anti-interference ability, and the sensor was used to measure the amount of DA in commercial DA injection solutions with recoveries ranged from 99 to 103%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite NTO NPs
Dopamine
Ascorbic acid
Cyclic voltammetry
Differential pulse voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of NTONPs/MGCE and electrochemical measurements
Redox active surface area of BGCE and NTO NPs/MGCE
Effect of scan rate at NTO NPs /MGCE
Interference investigation: Detection of DA in the presence of AA
Reproducibility and stability of NTO NPs/MGCE sensor probe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), New Delhi, India, for the award of Junior Research fellowship [ 09/1207(0566)/2020-EMR-I ], and also very thankful to the Energy Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Siddaganga Institute of Technology for characteristics facility, and gratitude towards the principal, Siddaganga Institute of Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared NTO NPs.
FT-IR spectra of as-prepared NTO powders.
a. Diffuse reflectance spectra and b. plot of [F(R)]2 versus Energy showing the energy bandgap of NTO Nps.
a, b. FE-SEM images and c. EDS spectrum of the as-prepared NTO NPs with composition.
a-d. Elemental mapping of NaTaO3 nanoparticles. Elemental mapping of a. mix, b. oxygen, c. sodium, and d. Tantalum.
a, b. Morphological features of NTO NPs TEM Micrographs show different magnifications. c. HRTEM image and d. SAED pattern.
CVs of 25 mM K3 [Fe (CN)6] in 1 M KCl at BGCE (i) and NTO NPs /MGCE (ii) with the sweep rate of 50 mVs−1 .
Cyclic Voltammograms of 2.5 mM DA in 0.2 M PBS (pH 6.6) with a sweep rate of 50 mVs−1.

a. Cyclic Voltammograms of 2.5 mM DA recorded at NTO NPs/MGCE in 0.2 M PBS of different buffer pH values (5.8–7.8) having the sweep rate of 50mVs− 1. b. Plot of Epa of DA v/s different buffer pH values. c. Plot of Ipa of DA v/s pH.
a. Cyclic Voltammograms of 2.5 mM DA in 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6 with sweep rates (50–500) mVs−1 at NTO NPs/MGCE. b. Plot of log Ip of DA v/s log ν.
Cyclic Voltammograms for different concentrations of DA.
DPVs for different concentrations of DA (0.5 – 5 µM) in 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6 having the sweep rate of 50 mVs-1 c. Plot of Ipa versus concentration of DA for DPV.

a. DPVs for different concentrations of AA (10––80 mM) in 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6 with 10 µM DA and Scan rate of 50mVs−1 at NTO NPs/ MGCE. b. DPVs of DA (10–90 µM) in 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6 with 10 mM AA and Scan rate of 50mVs−1 at NTO NPs/MGCE.

a. Cyclic voltammogram of five separate samples (with the same composition) of 25 µM DA in 0.2 M PBS at NTO NPs /MGCE with scan rate 50 mVs−1 . b. Cyclic voltammogram of NTO NPs /MGCE to 25 µM DA in 0.2 M PBS (pH 6.6) solution for ten continuous cycles c. Stability for 10 µM DA oxidation with NTO NPs/ MGCE for 5 days after storage (DPV method).
Preparation of NTO NPs/ MGCE and possible electrooxidation of DA.
Comparative analytical performance of different modifiedelectrodes for detection of DA.
Analysis of DA in Pharmaceutical Samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of NaTaO3 nanoparticles and fabrication of nanostructured NaTaO3 for detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Studies in Chemistry, Davangere University, Shivagangothri, 577 007 Davangere, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Studies in Chemistry, Davangere University, Shivagangothri, 577 007 Davangere, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Siddaganga Institute of Technology, Tumakuru, Karnataka 572 103, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Siddaganga Institute of Technology, Tumakuru, Karnataka 572 103, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein a novel electrochemical sensor based on NaTaO3 (NTO) nanoparticles (NPs) immobilized modified GCE is fabricated for sensitive and selective determination of dopamine in the presence of Ascorbic acid. The perovskite NTO NPs were synthesized from the facile hydrothermal method. The structural morphology and characterization of NTO NPs were examined by using powder XRD, FT-IR, UV-DRS, SEM, elemental mapping, EDS, and HR-TEM analysis. NTO NPs showed a fantastic synergistic impact on the electrochemical sensing of dopamine (DA) at very low concentrations. Under optimum conditions, The NTO NPs/MGCE exhibited good linear properties in the concentration range from 5 × 10-7 M to 5 µM for the quantitative analysis of DA with a limit of detection 0.17 µM. The developed NTO NPs/MGCE could provide a new platform for the routine study of DA and Ascorbic acid (AA) due to its outstanding reproducibility and repeatability, good anti-interference ability, and the sensor was used to measure the amount of DA in commercial DA injection solutions with recoveries ranged from 99 to 103%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the crucial neurotransmitters of mammal’s central nervous system in the brain that belongs to the catecholamine family [1]. That plays pivotal roles in the interaction between neurons, like regulating emotions, memory, pleasure, pain, motivation, and other executive functions. Extreme DA secretion induces failure in energy metabolism and causes untimely death. In another way, lack of DA leads to several neurological diseases such as senile dementia, Parkinsonism, Schizophrenia, and Huntington's disease [2–4]. Some specific types of food plants contain large amounts of ascorbic acid (AA), a water-soluble cytosolic chain-breaking vitamin with antioxidant properties [5,6]. It is essential for numerous biological functions, including the reduction of free radicals, the prevention of cancer, and the enhancement of immunity [7,8], and is also utilized to treat mental disorders, the common cold, and scurvy prevention [9]. Hence, AA is frequently employed as an antioxidant on a broad scale in foods, animal feed, and medicinal compositions [10,11]. Hence, monitoring of neurological behavior and the simultaneous determination of DA is significant in diagnostic research and analytical application. Therefore, it is essential to develop fast, selective, and sensitive methods of quantitative estimation of DA in the presence of AA.

The most widely used technique for the determination of dopamine in vivo microdialysisone of the important features responsible for the success of this technique in the neurochemical investigation is the development of sophisticated analytical techniques capable of accurate detection of extremely small quantities of the molecules of interest [12]. Several kinds of analytical techniques have been used to determine DA and AA detection such as chromatographic methods, mass spectroscopy [13], fluorescence methods [14], chemiluminescence [15], and electrochemical techniques [16,17]. However, these techniques have some drawbacks, such as protracted processing periods, expensive prices, and the need for sample pretreatment. They are probably not appropriate for routine analysis due to these drawbacks. Among these techniques, electrochemical detection techniques have attracted much attention in recent years due to their high accuracy, rapid, portability, and modification with simple instrumentation. However, overlapping the voltammetric peaks of DA with AA is the main impediment to the precise and simultaneous quantification of DA and AA. And the pronounced fouling in the electrode always results in poor selectivity and repeatability [18]. To address these issues, electrodes have been modified with materials including polymer films [19], noble metal nanoparticles [20], metal oxide [21,22], and carbonaceous materials [23–26].

Recently, there has been a lot of interest in the field of sensor research on the development of novel electrochemical sensors that use different nanoparticles and nanocomposites to detect different chemical compounds and toxicsubstances [27–35]. Mixed oxides of the perovskite-type nanoparticles have excellent physicochemical properties and are being extensively researched for their potential applications in various fields [36–38]. One such attractive characteristic is their greater surface-to-volume ratio, which generates a lot of active sites and has an impact on enhancing molecule adsorption and electrocatalytic activity and makes analytical equipment based on them exceedingly sensitive, affordable, and selective [39]. Perovskite oxides have recently been employed as working electrodes for electrochemical sensing by M. Shashank et al., battery applications, humidity sensor based on NaTaO3 nanocrystaline by Y. Zhang et al., and photocatalytic degradation, Visible-light photooxidation of ciprofloxacin by A. Shawky et al., U. Farooq et al. studied the High-surface-area sodium tantalate nanoparticles with enhanced photocatalytic and electrical properties prepared through polymeric citrate precursor route, electron bridging based on ternary AgNPs/MoS2/NaTaO3 heterojunction films enhanced photocatalytic activity has been studied Y. Zhang et al., L. Tang et al., studied the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ternary Ag/g-C3N4/NaTaO3 photocatalysts, Enhanced photocatalytic water splitting hydrogen production on RuO2/La: NaTaO3 prepared by sol–gel method studied by L. M. Torres-Martínez et al., [36,40–47] owing to their improved electrocatalytic activity. Here, the Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with NaTaO3 nanoparticles (NTO NPs) is used as a novel electrochemical sensor for the detection of DA in the presence of AA. Since conductive GCE carries negative charges, positively charged DA can be selectively absorbed by keeping away negatively charged AA via electrostatic attraction [48]. Hence, in this study, we explore the DA sensing capability of NaTaO3 under various experimental conditions, such as pH and scan rate.

Every step of the analysis was conducted using only analytical grade reagents. Ascorbic acid, Sodium hydroxide, and potassium chloride, potassium ferricyanide were collected from HI media laboratories (Mumbai, India) with the highest available purity. DA, Tantalum (V) chloride was purchased from Sigma Ltd (Bangalore, India). The supporting electrolyte of 0.2 M phosphate buffer solution was prepared by using a mixture of 0.2 M of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. The water used to prepare all aqueous solutions was doubly demineralized.

All the evaluations were performed at room temperature. Rigaku smart lab X–ray diffractometer (XRD) was used for crystal structure analysis. Chemical bonding interactions were explored with the Bruker-Alpha FT-IR spectrophotometer from the KBr pellet method. LAB INDIA UV 3092 spectrophotometer was utilized to evaluate the optical properties. The surface morphology was analyzed using (FESEM- CARLZEISS Ultra 55) Scanning electron microscope, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to describe the nanoparticle's size, shape, and internal structures.

Elemental and trace elemental analyses were made by using EDAX (Oxford INCAx-act) and elemental mapping. A CHI-660E voltammetric analyzer (CH-instrument-660E electrochemical workstation) was employed for CV, DPV, and LSV techniques. The electrolytic cell with a three-electrode system comprised of a counter electrode (platinum wire), a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl electrode in KCl), and working electrodes (bare and NTO NPs modified glassy carbon electrodes).

Compared to the traditional high-temperature reaction, the hydrothermal approach is obviously the cheapest, energy-saving, and simple method to synthesize crystalline NTO with nano size. 0.2 g TaCl5 powder dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and this solution was dispersed in 10 M NaOH solution and stirred on a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for about 60 min, then obtained white-colored solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (capacity 100 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 24 hr. The white precipitate obtained was subjected to washing twice with distilled water followed by ethanol and dried at 80 °C for 12 hr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of NTONPs/MGCE and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to modifying the electrodes, GCE was polished well with 0.05 µM alumina slurry and washed with de-ionized water several times to remove the undesirable alumina particles from the surface of GCE. The required weight of NTO NPs (5 mg/mL) was taken and re-dispersed using de-ionized water and then it was coated by the drop coating method and dried at room temperature. Washing the MGCE with de-ionized water eliminates the unbounded molecules from the surface of the GCE. The preparation of NTO NPs/ MGCE was outlined in Scheme 1 . After the successful fabrication of cubic-shaped NTO NPs on GCE was further used to investigate the DA sensing performance.

The electrochemical cell was filled with 10 mL of 0.2 M PBS (pH 6.6) and a certain amount ofdopamine solution, subsequently, the three-electrode system was incorporated into it. Cyclic voltammetry was performed in the potential window ranging from –0.2 to 0.8 V with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1. DPV was carried out in the potential window range from -0.2 to 0.8 V with a pulse amplitude of 50 mV and pulse width of 0.05 s [49,50].

The XRD pattern of the obtained pure perovskite NTO nanomaterials is presented in Fig. 1 . It can be seen that all reflection peaks for this sample may be attributed to a pure monoclinic phase of crystalline NTO. There are nine obvious diffraction peaks in this pattern. The diffraction peaks (100), (110), (−111), (200), (−210), (−211), (−202) (−221), and (310) at different d-spacing can be indexed as pure perovskite structure (NTO) according to the standard card (JCPDS-ICDD card No. 74–2479), which demonstrated that NTO was the only phase created during the process. The greater crystalline character of NTO is revealed by the sharp XRD peak. Crystallite sizes can be estimated from the XRD patterns through Scherer equation dhkl = 0.9λ/(β cos θB) (1), where d is the average crystallite size, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray, β is the full width at half-maximum, and θB is Bragg’s angle [51]. The average crystallite size was calculated using this equation and found to be 45 nm.

The chemical functionalization of nanocrystalline NTO samples was investigated using FT-IR analysis as shown in Fig. 2 . The fingerprint peak responsible for TaO vibrations was observed at 643 cm−1. This TaO band in the spectra was observed due to the TaO stretching and TaOTa bridge stretching modes. The symmetric combination of TaO stretching modes is the reason responsible for these bands [52]. The IR peak at 3421 cm−1 corresponds to the OH vibrations of the adsorbed water on the surface.

The NTO NPs' photo absorbance was examined using a UV–vis spectrometer. Fig. 3 a shows the typical diffuse reflection spectra of NTO, the intense absorption peak observed in the wavelength between 198 and 304 nm, indicates that the NTO was capable of absorbing strong UV radiation with high absorbance implying the material is capably employed as an organic UV blocker rather than organic absorber. This band arises due to the transition of electrons from the conduction band to the valence band. As known, for a crystalline semiconductor, the optical absorption near the band edge follows equation (1) [53].
(1)
α = A
( h υ -
E g
)
n / 2
/ h υ
where α, υ, Eg, and n are the absorption coefficient, incident light frequency, band gap, constant, and integer, respectively. Among them, n decides the characteristics of the transition in a semiconductor. The optical transition for NTO is directly allowed hence by using this equation the n value was 1 for NTO nanocubes. The band gap (Eg) of the NTO nanocubes could be estimated to be 3.6 eV as shown in Fig. 3b obtained by lotting energy (eV) of photons versus [F(R)]2 and Eg for the prepared nanoparticles were calculated by using the Kubelka-Munk hypothesis. The corresponding intercept value gives the energy band gap value of NTO. The function F(R) and photon energy (E) were evaluated by using the expressions (2) and (3).
(2)
F
R
=
( 1 - R )
2
/ 2 R
(3)
E = hc / λ = 1240.8 / λ
eV
where R is the reflection factor, λ is the wavelength of absorption (nm).

Fig. 4 (a, b) demonstrates the SEM images of NTO NPs exhibit cubic morphology. As known, NTO is an ABO3 perovskite-type structure, where TaO6 tetrahedra are coupled in a corner-sharing arrangement, allowing the establishment of cubic morphology [54]. The elemental composition of the as-prepared NTO was estimated by the EDS aspect given in Fig. 4c. which displays the EDS spectrum derived for NTO NPs, indicating the presence and consistency of O, Na, and Ta with atomic percentages (showed in the inset Fig. 4c). The uniform distribution of the elements in the prepared NTO sample was confirmed by the elemental mapping with colors red, green, and, blue for the elements O, Na, and Ta respectively, as outlined in Fig. 5 (a-d).

TEM images of the NTO nanocubes at different magnifications are displayed in Fig. 6 (a,b), and the HR-TEM image shown in Fig. 6c gives clear information about the d spacing value is 0.30 nm, which belongs to the plane (110). The crystallinity of the NTO nanocubes was confirmed with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of HRTEM as shown in Fig. 6(d) and also with bright circular fringes in SAED pattern closely matches the d-spacing values estimated from XRD data having the highest intensity planes at (110), (100) and (210) [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Redox active surface area of BGCE and NTO NPs/MGCE
ce:section.ce:para:
The CV investigation of 25 mM K3 [Fe (CN)6] in 1.0 M KCl with the sweep rate of 50 mVs−1 was studied at both BGCE and NTO NPs/MGCE. Fig. 7 .(i) dotted line & (ii) Solid line depicts the cyclic voltammograms recorded at BGCE and NTO NPs/MGCE respectively. The redox peak separation (ΔEp) values and anodic peak current are found to be 0.463 V & 1.4 µA for BGCE and 0.378 V & 1.9 µA for MGCE respectively. The peak currents are increased significantly for modified electrodes with decreasing the redox peak separation value. These results indicate increased electron transfer rate and electroactive sites. Using the CV technique and data from the literature [54] the active surface area of NTO NPs/ MGCE and BGCE is calculated by using Randles- Sevick's equation Eq. (4).
(4)
I p
=
( 2.69 ×
10 5
)
n
3 / 2
A
0
D 0
1 / 2
C
0
ν
1 / 2
The calculated active surface area was found to be 0.0273 cm2 for BGCE and 0.038 cm2 for NTO NPs/MGCE [53].

The electroanalytical response of 2.5 mM DA in 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6 has been studied with a sweep rate of 50 mVs−1 at both BGCE and NTO NPs/ MGCE. By comparing both voltammograms we noticed that at BGCE the anodic peak current (Ipa) was 7.1 μA with a peak potential (Epa) of 0.537 V was shown with a dotted line in Fig. 8 . (i) and the anodic peak current (Ipa) was found to be 8.6 μA with peak potential (Epa) of 0.478 V at NTO NPs /MGCE is showed with a solid line in Fig. 8. (ii). The increased roughness and surface area trigger the noticeable enhancement in sensitivity and selectivity of the NTO NPs /MGCE. As a result, NTO NPs /MGCE were employed for further investigation.

In the electroanalytical investigation to attain an acceptable sensitivity and to studythe redox behavior of the targeted analytes, the pH of supporting media must be optimized The CV approach was employed to determine the pH-dependent electrochemical profile of DA at the NTO NPs/ MGCE in the range from pH 5.8 to pH 7.8. The acidity of the electrolyte affected the peak potential of DA because of the involvement of protons in the surface reaction. The voltammetric signals of 2.5 mM DA in 0.2 M PBS with a sweep rate of 50 mVs−1 were recorded at NTO NPs/ MGCE. Fig. 9 a displays the influence of pH on the electrochemical oxidation of DA. Fig. 9b shows the peak potential shifts to a less positive side with an increase in pH indicating the involvement of the proton-coupled electro-oxidation process of DA at the surface of the sensor accompanied by an equal number of electrons and protons will be equal to two, the possible reaction mechanism involves electrooxidation of DA is shown in Scheme 1 [55–61]. The effect of pH was studied by plotting the graph of pH versus Ipa The maximum Ipa was obtained at pH 6.6 as shown in Fig. 9c, which was optimized for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate at NTO NPs /MGCE
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of the reaction were investigated by the impact of scan rate on the peak currents and peak potentials of DA with the help of the CV technique. Fig. 10 a interprets the voltammograms of 2.5 mM DA in 0.2 M PBS (pH = 6.6) at different sweep rates varying from 50 to 500 mVs−1, it clearly shows that oxidation peak currents progressively increased with the increment in sweep rates and oxidation peak potential slightly shifted towards the positive side. A linear relationship is established between the logarithmic anodic peak current and the logarithmic scan rate depicted in Fig. 10b with linear regression equation expressed as log Ipa = 0.4562 logν mVs−1 + 0.2257; R2 = 0.9978. The ideal electrochemical reaction governed by the diffusion-controlled process has a theoretical value of 0.5 and the experimental slope will be found to be 0.456, which is closer to that of the anticipated value confirming the electrode behavior was diffusion-controlled sensor process [62,63].

DPV technique was employed to study the Quantification of DA. The NTO NPs /MGCE were used to detect the DA in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.6 V. In these measurements, the concentration of DA is varied in the dynamic range of 0.5 to 5 µM in 0.2 M PBS at pH 6.6 with a scan rate of 50mVs−1. From Figs. 11a and b
, it was observed that the Oxidation peak current increases with the increase in the concentration of DA. The plot of a calibration curve, Ip v/s C (μM) showed good linearity with the regression equation Ip = 0.1789C + 0.4171 (R2 = 0.9986). The limit of detection and quantification limits were proposed by using the formulae 6 and 7 respectively [64]. The estimated values of LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.179 μM & and 0.597 μM respectively.
(5)
LOD = 3 S / M
(6)
LOQ = 10 S / M
Where S denotes the standard deviation and M is the slope obtained from calibration plots. The efficacy of the fabricated sensor compared with other reported literature is shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference investigation: Detection of DA in the presence of AA
ce:section.ce:para: DPV is more sensitive than CV since it was designed to reduce the background capacitive current that occurs at the electrode surface due to the formation of an electrical double layer of an electrochemical sensor [65]. The anti-interference capability was performed in the mixture of samples of DA and AA at the NTO NPs/MGCE using the DPV technique by keeping one of the constituents of the mixture at a fixed concentration and varying another one. Fig. 12 a shows the DPV responses for different concentrations of AA from 10 to 80 mM (a-h) in the presence of 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6 with the sweep rate of 50 mVs−1 at NTO NPs/MGCE by keeping the constant concentration of DA (10 × 10-6M). Further, the concentration of DA is varied in the range of 10 µM to 90 µM (a-i) at the constant concentration of AA (10 mM) the corresponding plot is shown in Fig. 12b. As observed from the figure the peak current rapidly increases with increase in the concentration of respective analyte, is due to higher current sensitivity and lack of background current [49].

The practical application of the fabricated sensor was illustrated by using commercially available dopamine hydrochloride injection with a specified content of DA 40.0 mg/mL. The injection samples were prepared to 2.5 mM (suitable dilution in 0.2 M PBS of pH 6.6). The standard addition method was used for the determination of DA concentration in the pharmaceutical preparations and to analyze the samples based on the repeated Linear Sweep Voltammetry responses (n = 3) for the diluted samples that were spiked with the specified concentration of DA. The results are given in Table 2 . Indicated that the recoveries of the spiked samples were in the range from 99% to 103%. Therefore, the proposed sensor could be applied for the real sample analysis with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of NTO NPs/MGCE sensor probe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reproducibility of the novel NTO NPs/MGCE, the current responses of five different electrode samples towards a 25 µM DA in 0.2 M PBS (pH 6.6) solution were examined (Fig. 13 a). The current response relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 0.76, demonstrating good reproducibilityof the NTO NPs/MGCE. To examine the operational stability of NTO NPs /MGCE, the sensor was continuously scanned for 10 consecutive cycles using a solution containing 25 µM DA in 0.2 M PBS at pH 6.6 with a sweep rate of 50 mVs−1. % degradation = (Ipn/Ipl) 100, where Ipn is the peak current at the final cycle and Ipl is the peak current at the first cycle, is a formula used to compute the percentage of degradation. The results confirmed the electrode's good stability by showing that 84% of the initial current response was retained as shown in Fig. 13b. The peak current intensity was measured by the DPV method in 10 µM DA shown in Fig. 13c. No conspicuous change was noticed and it retained 93% of its initial current response after storage of 5 indicating good storage stability of the DA sensor with % RSD is found to be 2.48% [70–72].

In this present work, the cubic NaTaO3 nanoparticles are synthesized through a facile hydrothermal approach. The synthesized nanocubes were used in the fabrication of NTO NPs/MGCE for further investigation of various parameters electrochemically using cyclic and differential pulse voltammetric techniques. The NTO NPs/MGCE revealed excellent electrocatalytic activity for sensing and low-level detection limits of DA. The NTO NPs/MGCE were found promising electrode materials that could be used to detect DA selectively in the presence of probable additional interference AA. The NTO NPs/MGCE can be successfully used to determine dopamine in the real sample with acceptable recovery. The modified electrode also exhibited good reproducibility and stability in the sensing of DA and AA.

K.M. Pallavi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. G.P. Mamatha: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. G. Nagaraju: . T.L. Soundarya: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), New Delhi, India, for the award of Junior Research fellowship [09/1207(0566)/2020-EMR-I], and also very thankful to the Energy Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Siddaganga Institute of Technology for characteristics facility, and gratitude towards the principal, Siddaganga Institute of Technology.

Chem. Rev.
